event: Pre-ceramic Amerindian peoples, likely from South America, become the earliest known inhabitants of the island later called Saint Barthelemy.
Arawak-speaking Saladoid migrants from the Orinoco region settle the Lesser Antilles, establishing ceramic-using fishing and gathering communities in the area.
Carib peoples expand through the Lesser Antilles, and the small, arid island is used seasonally rather than permanently settled.
Christopher Columbus sights the island during his second voyage and names it San Bartolome after his brother Bartolomeo.
France formally takes possession of the island, with settlers arriving from nearby Saint Kitts under the French colonial sphere.
The Knights of Malta acquire the island along with other French Caribbean holdings.
Carib raiders attack and destroy the small French settlement, temporarily depopulating the island of Europeans.
French colonists re-establish a permanent settlement on the island.
The island passes to the French West India Company as part of the reorganization of France's Caribbean possessions.
Saint Barthelemy is attached directly to the French crown and administratively linked to Guadeloupe.
British forces briefly occupy the island during the War of the Austrian Succession.
France regains the island under the Treaty of Paris ending the Seven Years' War.
France cedes Saint Barthelemy to Sweden in exchange for trading rights in the port of Gothenburg.
Sweden takes formal possession and renames the main town Gustavia after King Gustav III.
Gustavia is declared a free port, spurring rapid growth as a neutral trading and transshipment hub.
The Swedish West India Company is chartered to manage trade and administration of the colony.
Wartime neutrality and the free port turn Gustavia into a thriving commercial center serving Caribbean and American merchants.
Britain briefly occupies Saint Barthelemy during the Napoleonic Wars before returning it to Sweden.
Slavery on the island is regulated under a Swedish ordinance amid the colony's commercial decline.
A devastating fire sweeps through Gustavia, accelerating the town's economic decline.
Sweden abolishes slavery in its colonies, freeing the enslaved population of Saint Barthelemy.
Another major fire and a hurricane further damage Gustavia, deepening the island's economic stagnation.
A referendum among the island's inhabitants overwhelmingly favors a return to French rule.
Sweden sells Saint Barthelemy back to France, ending nearly a century of Swedish sovereignty.
France reattaches the island to the colony of Guadeloupe as a dependent commune.
A powerful hurricane strikes the island, causing widespread destruction and prompting further emigration.
Guadeloupe, including Saint Barthelemy, becomes a French overseas department, granting residents full French citizenship.
American philanthropist David Rockefeller buys property on the island, marking the beginning of upscale tourism interest.
Gustaf III Airport opens, improving access and laying groundwork for the island's tourism economy.
Luxury tourism expands rapidly as wealthy visitors and exclusive resorts transform the island's economy.
Hurricanes Luis and Marilyn strike the island in quick succession, causing major damage to infrastructure and tourism facilities.
A local referendum approves separating Saint Barthelemy from Guadeloupe to form a distinct French overseas entity.
Saint Barthelemy officially becomes a separate French overseas collectivity, gaining its own territorial council.
Bruno Magras is elected the first president of the new Territorial Council of Saint Barthelemy.
Saint Barthelemy changes its status with the European Union to become an Overseas Country and Territory rather than an outermost region.
Hurricane Irma devastates Saint Barthelemy as a Category 5 storm, causing extensive damage across the island.
Xavier Lederlin is elected president of the Territorial Council, succeeding long-serving leader Bruno Magras.
